title: Global May Day Rallies Target War-Driven Cost-of-Living Crisis
summary: Across the globe on Friday, May Day rallies transformed from traditional labor celebrations into urgent protests against the economic fallout of the Iran war. Activists in dozens of countries linked soaring energy costs and shrinking purchasing power directly to the conflict, demanding higher wages, peace, and government action. "Working people refuse to pay the price for Donald Trump's war in the Middle East," the European Trade Union Confederation declared, representing 93 union organizations across 41 countries. "Today's rallies show working people will not stand by and see their jobs and living standards destroyed." In Manila, large crowds marched toward the U.S. Embassy, some carrying banners reading "no troops, no bases, no war games, resist U.S.-led wars." Protesters clashed with police blocking their path. Josua Mata, leader of the SENTRO labor federation, told reporters, "Every Filipino worker now is aware that the situation here is deeply connected to the global crisis." In Jakarta, Indonesian President Prabowo Subianto made a rare appearance at a May Day rally, greeting tens of thousands amid tight security. Workers called for stronger protections against rising prices and raw material shortages. In Islamabad, 55-year-old construction worker Mohammad Maskeen captured the desperation: "How will I bring vegetables and other necessities home if I don't work?" European demonstrations carried similar urgency. In Paris, unions marched under the slogan "bread, peace and freedom," explicitly linking workers' daily struggles to conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East. In Istanbul, police detained about 15 protesters attempting to reach Taksim Square, the symbolic heart of 2013 anti-government demonstrations. Governments responded with mixed signals. Italy approved nearly €1 billion in job incentives ahead of May Day, extending tax breaks to encourage hiring young people and disadvantaged women — a package opposition parties dismissed as "pure propaganda." In Portugal, nine months of negotiations over proposed labor law changes remain deadlocked, with unions warning the reforms would weaken workers' rights. France saw heated debate over a parliamentary proposal to allow work on May 1, the country's most protected public holiday — the only day when most employees have a mandatory paid day off. The proposal sparked major outcry from unions and left-leaning politicians, underscoring how deeply symbolic this day remains.
why_it_matters: When wars disrupt global energy and food supplies, teenagers may face higher prices for basic goods and fewer job opportunities in the future, making foreign policy decisions a direct influence on everyday life far from any battlefield.

keywords.explanation: The amount of goods and services your money can actually buy; when prices rise, purchasing power falls.
An organization made up of many smaller groups or unions that join together for common goals.
A group of smaller labor unions that have combined into a larger, more powerful body.
A situation where negotiations cannot move forward because both sides are stuck and cannot agree.
Something that represents an idea or a feeling without being the actual thing itself.
Information, often biased or one-sided, used to promote a particular political cause or viewpoint.

questions.question: What conflict did the global May Day protests link to the cost-of-living crisis?
options: C. A trade dispute with China

questions.question: Which organization declared that 'working people refuse to pay the price for Donald Trump's war in the Middle East'?
options: B. The European Trade Union Confederation
questions.correct_answer: B. The European Trade Union Confederation

questions.question: In which city did protesters clash with police while trying to reach the U.S. Embassy?
Who made a rare appearance at a May Day rally in Jakarta?
options: B. The head of the labor federation

questions.question: What happened to about 15 protesters in Istanbul?
options: B. They were detained by police
C. They made speeches at Taksim Square
D. They joined the president's rally
questions.correct_answer: B. They were detained by police

questions.question: What did Italy approve shortly before May Day?
options: A. A new tax on workers
B. A ban on public demonstrations
C. Nearly €1 billion in job incentives
D. A law allowing work on May 1
questions.correct_answer: C. Nearly €1 billion in job incentives

background_read: May Day, known as International Workers' Day, traces back to the 1886 Haymarket affair in Chicago, where workers demanded an eight-hour workday. It has since become a global day of protest, often mixing labor rights with anti-war sentiments. During the 2003 Iraq War, millions marched on May 1 across Europe and the United States, linking military spending to cuts in domestic programs.

The article portrays a scenario where a conflict in Iran drives up energy costs, echoing historical patterns: wars in the Middle East have repeatedly triggered oil price shocks, such as the 1973 oil crisis, squeezing household budgets worldwide. The European Trade Union Confederation, an umbrella group that speaks for millions of workers across 41 countries, is one of the most powerful labor voices in Europe.

In the Philippines, SENTRO is a prominent labor federation that has often organized against what it sees as U.S. military influence. Taksim Square in Istanbul holds deep symbolic meaning for Turks; it was the epicenter of the 2013 Gezi Park protests against government policies, and authorities still tightly control demonstrations there.

Prabowo Subianto, a former general who became Indonesia's president in 2024 after a long political career, made his May Day appearance notable because Indonesian leaders have rarely attended such rallies, signaling a potential shift in labor relations. In France, May 1 has been a fully protected paid holiday since 1947, making any proposal to allow work on that day politically explosive.
Article_Structure: LEAD: May Day rallies transform from traditional labor celebrations to protests against a war-driven cost-of-living crisis
GLOBAL FRAMING: The European Trade Union Confederation directly blames the Iran war and Trump
├─ MANILA: March toward U.S. Embassy, clashes with police, 'resist U.S.-led wars'
├─ JAKARTA: President Prabowo makes rare rally appearance, workers demand protection
├─ ISLAMABAD: Construction worker voices desperation over food prices
├─ PARIS: 'Bread, peace and freedom' slogan links daily struggles to conflicts
└─ ISTANBUL: Detentions near symbolic Taksim Square recall 2013 protests
├─ ITALY: €1 billion in job incentives approved, but opposition calls it 'pure propaganda'
├─ PORTUGAL: Labor law negotiations deadlocked for nine months, unions warn of weakened rights
└─ FRANCE: Heated debate over a proposal to allow work on the most protected public holiday
UNDERLYING TENSION: The protests show workers refusing to bear the economic burden of war

perspectives.description: Workers see the global protests as a powerful way to demand higher wages and peace. By uniting across borders, they believe they can pressure governments to prioritize domestic spending over military conflicts, and Italy's job incentives show that such pressure can prompt at least symbolic concessions.

Some governments dismiss the rallies as ineffective or manipulative; Italy's opposition called the job package 'pure propaganda.' In Portugal and France, proposed reforms aim to make labor markets more flexible, but unions fear this will weaken hard-won protections and silence workers' voices.

May Day has historically fused labor and anti-war movements, reflecting a pattern where international crises magnify domestic inequality. The current protests illustrate how a distant conflict can quickly ripple into supermarket prices, yet history suggests that such demonstrations rarely change military policy quickly.

If food and energy costs keep rising, more countries could see similar unrest, potentially influencing elections and forcing lawmakers to choose between war funding and social safety nets. The outcome of France's debate over May 1 work and Portugal's labor deadlock may signal whether worker protections strengthen or erode in this climate.
